name: Cerro San Quilez and Piedra Molón Trail
name_original: Cerro San Quilez y Piedra Molón (PR-CU 118) — circular desde Valdeolivas
description: Hike the moderate 4.8-mile Cerro San Quilez and Piedra Molón Trail to explore olive groves, medieval Valdeolivas, and unique rock formations
region_intro: Hiking around Alcarria offers diverse landscapes shaped by rivers, limestone, and gypsum formations. The region features a plateau with undulating strata, deep river valleys, and impressive gorges carved by rivers like the Tagus and Dulce. Hikers can explore various karst phenomena and extensive lavender fields, particularly around Brihuega. The Sierra de Altomira provides elevated views and well-preserved Mediterranean forests, contributing to a varied hiking experience. Best hiking trails around Alcarria The most popular hiking route is Ermita de Nuestra Señora de los Desamparados – circular along the Guadiela River (PR-CU 47), a 8.0 miles (12.8 km) trail that takes 3 hours 27 minutes to complete, following the Guadiela River. Another top favourite among local hikers is Route of the Faces - circular from Buendía (PR-CU 46), a moderate 9.4 miles (15.2 km) path, known for its unique rock formations and scenic views. Local hikers also love the Cerro de la Degollada – circular from Priego, a 5.9 miles (9.5 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 3 hours 54 minutes. Hiking around Alcarria is defined by river valleys, gorges, karst formations, and Mediterranean forests, offering a network of routes suitable for various ability levels. The routes in Alcarria are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 400 hikers have used komoot to explore Alcarria's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Alcarria?
A: Alcarria offers a wide selection of hiking opportunities, with over 30 routes available. These trails cater to various preferences, ranging from easy strolls to more challenging treks through the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: What are the different difficulty levels for hiking in Alcarria?
A: The hiking trails in Alcarria are categorized into three main difficulty levels: there are 8 easy routes, 21 moderate routes, and 3 difficult routes. This variety ensures that hikers of all experience levels can find a suitable path.

Q: When is the best time of year to go hiking in Alcarria?
A: The best time to hike in Alcarria largely depends on what you wish to experience. For the vibrant spectacle of the lavender fields, typically from June to August, the area around Brihuega is particularly stunning. Spring and autumn offer milder temperatures, ideal for exploring the river valleys, gorges, and Mediterranean forests comfortably.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking trails in Alcarria?
A: Yes, Alcarria has several easy routes suitable for families. These trails often feature gentle terrain and offer opportunities to explore natural beauty without extensive effort. Consider routes that pass through charming towns or offer views of the region's unique geological formations.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Alcarria?
A: Many of Alcarria's trails are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012673371/ermita-de-nuestra-senora-de-los-desamparados-circular-along-the-guadiela-river-pr-cu-47" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita de Nuestra Señora de los Desamparados – circular along the Guadiela River (PR-CU 47)&lt;/a&gt; is a popular option, offering a scenic loop along the Guadiela River. Another great choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1242639649/altomira-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Altomira Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which provides extensive views from the Sierra de Altomira.

Q: Are there any trails that feature unique natural landmarks or geological formations?
A: Absolutely. Alcarria is known for its diverse geological features. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1012671860/route-of-the-faces-circular-from-buendia-pr-cu-46" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Faces - circular from Buendía (PR-CU 46)&lt;/a&gt; is famous for its unique rock sculptures. You can also explore karst phenomena in areas like Las Majadas or La Ciudad Encantada, or visit the distinctive twin mountains known as the Tetas de Viana.

Q: What kind of views can I expect on Alcarria's hiking trails?
A: Hikers in Alcarria can enjoy a variety of scenic views. The Sierra de Altomira offers spectacular 360-degree panoramas of the entire region. Many trails follow river valleys, providing picturesque vistas of gorges carved by rivers like the Tagus and Dulce. You can also find viewpoints such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6503495" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cruz de los Borrachos Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any waterfalls to see while hiking in Alcarria?
A: Yes, the region features beautiful waterfalls. The Civica Waterfall along the Tajuña river is a notable scenic spot. Additionally, the town of Trillo, often called the 'door to the Alto Tajo,' is famous for its spectacular waterfalls, offering a refreshing sight during your hike.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy the most about hiking in Alcarria?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 40 reviews. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, from river valleys and gorges to karst formations and Mediterranean forests, and the well-maintained network of routes suitable for various ability levels.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions near the hiking trails?
A: Alcarria is rich in history and culture. You can find charming towns like Brihuega with historical sites such as the church of San Felipe, or Cifuentes with its castle. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3235056" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of Our Lady of the Forsaken&lt;/a&gt; is another historical point of interest. The Monsalud Monastery in Corcoles also adds a cultural dimension to a hiking trip.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging routes for experienced hikers?
A: While many routes are moderate, Alcarria does offer more challenging options. The region is traversed by sections of larger routes such as 'El Camino del Cid' and the 'Eastern Tagus Nature Trail,' which cater to those seeking longer adventures. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1242031295/route-of-the-mirador-del-rodenal-pr-cu-42-circular-from-priego" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route of the Mirador del Rodenal (PR-CU 42) — circular from Priego&lt;/a&gt; is classified as difficult, offering a substantial elevation gain and a rewarding experience for advanced hikers.

Q: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the trails?
A: Alcarria's well-preserved Mediterranean forests, particularly in areas like the Sierra de Altomira, are home to diverse flora and fauna. Hikers might spot various bird species, including royal eagles, booted eagles, and griffon vultures, making it an excellent region for nature observation.
tips: The hermitage is located on Cerro Quílez. From its viewpoint we can enjoy magnificent views of the Hoya de Infantado, a natural depression between the Sierras de Altomira and Bascuñana.
So called "La Piedra Molón" are the remains of a truncated conical grinding stone. From this point we can enjoy the spectacular views of the Sierras de Bascuñana and the Rodenal.
The parish church of Nuestra Señora de la Asunción is the main monument of Valdeolivas. It is a Romanesque temple from the 12th-13th centuries that has a beautiful tower.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Cerro San Quilez and Piedra Molón Trail?
faqs.answer: The trail is generally considered moderate by komoot, but local assessments often describe it as having a low difficulty level, making it accessible for many hikers. It includes a forest path and an ascent of nearly 200 meters over a 2-kilometer stretch at one point.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: The trail is a circular route starting and ending in Valdeolivas. You can typically find parking within the urban center of Valdeolivas, which serves as the trailhead for this route.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Cerro San Quilez and Piedra Molón Trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas or the urban center of Valdeolivas, to ensure their safety and respect for local wildlife and residents.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The trail can be enjoyed year-round. However, spring and autumn offer the most pleasant temperatures for hiking, with vibrant landscapes in spring and beautiful colors in autumn. Summer can be hot, so an early morning start is advisable, while winter hikes might require appropriate gear for cooler weather.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay any fees to hike this route?
faqs.answer: No, this is a public trail, and no special permits or entrance fees are required to access the Cerro San Quilez and Piedra Molón Trail.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail offers several notable features. You'll encounter the fascinating Piedra Molón, a unique half-carved millstone. The route also passes by the Hermitage of San Quirico and Santa Julita, offering panoramic views, and the historic Church of Our Lady of the Assumption in Valdeolivas with its 13th-century Romanesque paintings.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect on the hike?
faqs.answer: You'll experience a diverse landscape typical of the La Alcarria region. This includes picturesque olive groves, vineyards, and cereal fields. The trail also offers magnificent views of the Sierras de Bascuñana and El Rodenal, and passes through the charming, medieval-feeling urban center of Valdeolivas.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect with any other hiking paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with sections of other official trails. You will find it shares parts of its path with the Camino de Santiago de la Lana.BTT and the Camino de Santiago de la Lana.Senderista.

faqs.question: Is the trail suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Given its low to moderate difficulty and circular nature, the trail can be suitable for families. The varied scenery and historical points of interest like Piedra Molón and the medieval village of Valdeolivas can keep younger hikers engaged. Always consider your children's hiking experience and stamina.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Cerro San Quilez and Piedra Molón Trail?
faqs.answer: The terrain varies, featuring forest paths, dirt tracks through agricultural fields (olive groves, vineyards, cereal fields), and paved sections within the urban area of Valdeolivas. There is an ascent of nearly 200 meters over a 2-kilometer stretch, but overall, it's manageable.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like restaurants or cafes near the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail starts and ends in Valdeolivas, a small town that may offer limited amenities such as local bars or restaurants. It's advisable to check opening hours in advance or bring your own refreshments, especially during off-peak seasons.
